title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | City could face tax tug of war with maturing Mat-Su By David Reume (Published November 19, 2000) Yet another fiscal problem has poked its head over the time horizon of Alaska public affairs. The rapid growth of the Matanuska-Susitna area raises the possibility that a classic urban-suburban struggle will replay itself up here. I have in mind the struggle for tax base between central cities and their surrounding suburbs. Mild competition has long existed between Anchorage and the Valley. It may soon begin to intensify. Elsewhere in the country the urban-suburban struggle has begun something like this. Higher-income central city residents move out to the suburbs to breathe cleaner air, walk on safer streets and otherwise get more bang for their housing buck. People who leave the city te…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | All about image Graphicworks offers cutting edge in graphic displays By Mike Hinman Daily News Reporter (Published October 30, 2000) Huge banners touting exhibits in the Anchorage Museum of History and Art, every display at the Alaska Native Heritage Center, dozens of phone-company vehicles cruising Anchorage with logos emblazoned on them -- they all have one thing in common: Bonnie Moore. Moore, talking in a machine-gun pace, listed off some of the capabilities of her business, Graphicworks. The company can put images onto a wide variety of surfaces, from posters and backlit signs to fabric banners and fleet graphics. Moore, 35, said she runs into the same problem as many other businesses with newer technology. People don't know that fleet graphics, interpretive signs, museum displa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | BP Amoco earnings double Competitors also enjoy boost from high oil, gas prices Daily News Wire Services (Published November 8, 2000) London -- BP Amoco said Tuesday that third-quarter profits doubled thanks to sharply higher oil and natural gas prices and cost savings. The results were in line with expectations and followed similar strong results from BP Amoco's leading rivals, Exxon Mobil and Royal Dutch/Shell. Exxon said third-quarter profits nearly doubled to $4.3 billion, while Royal Dutch/Shell said profits rose 80 percent to $3.3 billion. BP is the second-largest oil producer in Alaska, where it operates the huge Prudhoe Bay field. The company also owns half of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline. BP's net income rose to $3.8 billion, or $16.91 cents a share, compared with $1.9 bill…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska must inform world on fish policy David Reaume Column (Published October 22, 2000) The struggle for position in the Canada-U.S. Pacific salmon negotiations spread into new territory last April. The Honorable Roger Simmons, consul general of Canada in Seattle, took his country's case to a large group of academic and government economists who had gathered in Bellingham, Wash., April 27-29 for the 34th annual Pacific Northwest Regional Economic Conference. To say that Simmons' prepared remarks and accusations were outrageous does little justice to their sheer audacity, hyperbole and inaccuracy. I was there with 70 or 80 other economists from the private sector and from universities and government agencies in British Columbia and the states of Washington, Idaho, Oregon and Californ…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska's Artists Going Global By Tony Hopfinger Daily News Reporter (Published October 20, 2000) This week's Alaska Federation of Natives Convention in Anchorage is one of the big opportunities of the year for artists to sell their work. But thanks to an ambitious Web site under development, they will eventually have the chance to market their arts and crafts to the world every day without leaving their villages. The Alaska Native Heritage Center in Anchorage is creating CultureNET, an Internet storefront for Alaska Native artists and a virtual gathering place to share cultural and educational information. The site will be like a hub with spokes branching to towns throughout Alaska, perhaps places such as Kotzebue, Ketchikan and Bethel, said Bobby Jo Kramer, development planner at th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published October 17, 2000) Brown Jug out of Fred's Fred Meyer is planning to install its own liquor store at its store on the corner of Muldoon and DeBarr Roads. Brown Jug Famous Liquor Stores is the current tenant but its lease runs out at the end of November and will not be renewed, said Fred Meyer spokesman Rob Boley. Brown Jug was given ample notice of Fred Meyer's plans, he said. A manager of the Muldoon Brown Jug confirmed the store is vacating but referred all questions to a public relations executive who could not be reached for comment. Shop gets AAA approval Cottman Transmission, 4510 Gambell St., is the first in Alaska to earn accreditation as an AAA approved drivetrain repair facility. The certification means that the company has met AAA's criteri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | New Businesses (Published October 18, 2000) Sunbok's Hair Design Sunbok Lee has opened Sunbok's Hair Design at her home. Hours are 9 a.m.-8 p.m. Monday-Saturday. She offers hair cuts, color, highlighting and wax treatments and more. Call 336-7010 or 223-9480 for location and information. Thai eatery opens Thai Orchid is a new restaurant in Anchorage, open Monday through Saturday for lunch and dinner. Hours are 11 a.m.-3 p.m. for lunch and 5-9 p.m. for dinner. The restaurant features freshly made exotic Thai cuisine and is located at 219 E. Dimond Boulevard in the Kings Row shopping center. Take-out service is also offered. Call 868-5226. Get help to get fit Michael Copenharve has established Exercise with Michael, a private health club. His goal is to help beginners start and maintai…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | FIELD NOTES (Published October 1, 2000) Club to walk Hilltop Ski Area Walkers in the Anchorage Volkssports Club plan to storm the Hilltop Ski Area on Saturday. The walk is scheduled for 9 a.m. to noon, starting at the Hilltop Haus outdoor deck. The walk is 10 kilometers on a No. 3 rated trail system along a dirt road and two major cross-country ski trails, including Spencer and Besh loops in the Hillside Ski Area. Spencer climbs about 300 feet; Besh is gently rolling. The walk is free. All are welcome, including dogs on leashes. Water and toilets will be available at the Hilltop Haus. There will be a drawing for a free pair of New Balance shoes and a special patch recognizing October as "National Voklkssporting Month" will be on sale. For more information, call J. Michael Devitt at 2…

title: Japan demand could increase
summary: Changes in the Japanese housing industry could bode well for Alaska's ailing timber industry, according to a new report commissioned by the state Department of Community and Economic Development. The report, by the Center for International Trade in Forest Products at the University of Washington, says revisions to Japan's national building code, combined with shifts in consumer preferences and building styles, have opened a door for products made from Alaska's Sitka spruce, western hemlock and red and yellow cedar. "With all this shake-up, it presents opportunities for Alaska manufacturers," said Michael Johnson, state trade specialist. "We have what they want," he said. Japan is Alaska's major export market. It buys about 70 percent of lumber products produced in the state and nearly all …

title: PROCESSOR ACCUSED OF ILLEGAL BUY
summary: Anchorage -- A Kenai seafood processor was indicted Wednesday by a federal grand jury on one count of conspiracy and four counts of buying illegally harvested halibut. The indictment was handed down against Kenai Custom Seafoods, owner James Hill Sr. and his son, James Hill Jr. Assistant U.S. attorney Kevin Feldis said the Hills knowingly bought halibut from a commercial fisherman who did not have an individual fishing quota required to catch halibut commercially. The alleged violations occurred between March and July of 1997. The indictment alleges that Kenai Custom Seafoods paid $42,000 for the halibut. Feldis said the company knew the fish was caught illegally, because the fisherman did not provide the required fish tickets and catch reports at the time of the sale. The Hills could face…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 'Free hemp' battle cry for local groups By Liz Ruskin Daily News Reporter (Published October 5, 2000) From a worn storefront office on Spenard Road, Al Anders is leading a crusade to legalize marijuana, and he is a fully committed zealot. Al Anders chats with a hemp supporter at the free hemp in Alaska offices Saturday in Spenard. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) He lives upstairs from the campaign office of Free Hemp in Alaska, in a commercial building just south of Chilkoot Charlie's. He has no car, no social life, no salary. He dresses in campaign T-shirts featuring Alaska flags with pot leaves in place of stars. He sometimes looks like he's not sleeping well. He's happy. "There's not a thing in the world I'd rather be doing than stopping the drug war," the Indiana native declar…

title: COAST GUARD BEGINS SEARCH FOR SHIP STOWAWAYS AT DUTCH
summary: Dutch Harbor -- A cargo ship suspected of containing Chinese stowaways arrived at the dock late Wednesday night and medics, Coast Guard officers with infrared video equipment, and agents with the U.S. Immigration and Naturalization Service began unloading the vessel to discover what it actually contained. The 860-foot Manoa tied up about 10 p.m. and the shipping company, APL, formerly American President Lines, immediately closed the area to the reporters and local residents who came to watch the unloading. "After going through a number of these situations, this is the quickest and safest way to resolve the situation," said APL spokesman John Pachtner about the tight dock security. "It's in the best interests of whoever, or whatever, is in that container." Soon after the vessel docked, Mike…

title: CHANGES IN MINORITY POPULATIONS BY STATE
summary: A state-by-state breakdown of 1999 population estimates - and percentage change since 1990 - of blacks, Hispanics, Asian and Pacific Islanders, American Indian and Alaska Natives as well as whites as provided by the Census Bureau. People of Hispanic descent can also be counted in any race.  ALABAMA: Blacks, 1,138,726, up 11.5 percent; Hispanics, 45,349, up 84.1 percent; Asian and Pacific Islanders, 28,435, up 29.7 percent; American Indian and Alaska Native, 14,599, down 11.9 percent; whites, 3,188,102, up 7.0 percent.  ALASKA: Blacks, 24,067, up 5.4 percent; Hispanics, 24,795, up 39.3 percent; Asian and Pacific Islanders, 28,040, up 38.7 percent; American Indian and Alaska Native, 101,352, up 17.5 percent; whites, 466,041, up 10.8 percent.  ARIZONA: Blacks, 175,506, up 52.7 percent; His…

title: SURGE STARTLES DISTRICT
summary: After dropping last year, enrollment in the Anchorage School District now appears to be surging by 700 to 1,000 students beyond the number expected. If the trend holds, it will leave the district scrambling to hire new teachers when school starts Wednesday. The district had been counting on about 49,300 kids this fall, about the same as last year. But planning director Norm Holthouse increased the estimate last week to 50,000 or more based on the number of students who registered for middle and high schools. He said the district won't know for sure until after Labor Day. Many families delay returning from vacation until then. Holthouse and state officials who monitor population shifts say more people must have been moving to Anchorage over the summer. But no one knows why or whether they'r…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | P&O Princess Cruises stock makes its debut By Gary Gentile The Associated Press (Published October 24, 2000) Los Angeles -- Shares of P&O Princess Cruises began floating on stock exchanges in London and New York on Monday after the company separated from the British conglomerate, P&O Group. P&O Princess Cruises PLC is the third-largest cruise company in the world, behind Carnival Cruise Lines and Royal Caribbean. As part of a "de-merger" announced earlier this year, shareholders of P&O Group received one share of P&O Princess Cruises for every share they held. The new company is based in London. U.S.-traded shares were listed Monday on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol POC. Shares finished Monday at $18 each. About 75 percent of the company's business will be in the…
